You can either run the notebooks in the Cookbook using 

Binder or on your local machine.

The simplest way to interact with a Jupyter Notebook is through


Binder, which enables “one click”
execution in the cloud. Simply navigate your mouse to
the top right corner of the book chapter you are viewing and click
on the rocket ship icon (see screenshots 

here),
and a text box will appear. Type or paste the Pythia Binder link
(https://binder.projectpythia.org) and click “Launch”.
After a few moments you should be presented with a
notebook that you can interact with. You’ll be able to execute code
and even change the example programs. At first the code cells
have no output, until you execute them by pressing
Shift+Enter. Complete details on how to interact with
a live Jupyter notebook are described in the Pythia Foundations chapter 

Getting Started with
Jupyter.

Note, not all Cookbook chapters are executable. If you do not see
the rocket ship icon, such as on this page, you are not viewing an
executable book chapter.

If you are interested in running this material locally on your computer, you will need to follow this workflow:

(Replace “cookbook-example” with the title of your cookbooks)

Clone the https://github.com/ProjectPythia/gdex-cookbook repository: git clone https://github.com/ProjectPythia/gdex-cookbook.git

Move into the gdex-cookbook directorycd gdex-cookbook

Create and activate your conda environment from the environment.yml fileconda env create -f environment.yml
conda activate gdex-cookbook

Move into the notebooks directory and start up Jupyterlabcd notebooks/
jupyter lab

GDEX (the NSF NCAR Research Data Archive) hosts petabytes of atmospheric, oceanic, and terrestrial datasets. This notebook introduces the GDEX REST API, explains how datasets are identified, and shows how to list and inspect available datasets.

Understand the GDEX dataset identifier (dsid) format

List all available datasets via the API

Retrieve basic metadata for a dataset

Check which access methods are available (ARCO, THREDDS, Globus, POSIX)

Concepts

Importance

Notes

Intro to the requests library

Helpful

Making HTTP calls

Familiarity with JSON

Helpful

Parsing API responses

Time to learn: 20 minutes

import requests
import json

All GDEX API endpoints live under https://gdex.ucar.edu/api/. Dataset identifiers use the format d###### (six digits), e.g. d633000 for ERA5.

# TODO: set the base URL and a target dataset ID
BASE_URL = "https://gdex.ucar.edu"
DSID = "d633000"  # ERA5 0.25-degree

GET /api/datasets/ returns a list of every dataset with its id and title. No authentication is required.

# TODO: call GET /api/datasets/ and print the first few results
# response = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/datasets/")
# data = response.json()
# print(data["data"]["count"], "datasets available")
# for ds in data["data"]["datasets"][:5]:
#     print(ds["id"], ds["title"])

Use the dataset-level metadata endpoints to learn about a specific dataset.

# TODO: fetch abstract, spatial coverage, and temporal coverage
# abstract = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/datasets/{DSID}/abstract/").json()
# spatial  = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/datasets/{DSID}/spatial_coverage/").json()
# temporal = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/datasets/{DSID}/temporal/").json()

GDEX datasets may be available via ARCO (Zarr), THREDDS, Globus, or direct download. Use GET /api/has_arco/{dsid}/ to check for cloud-optimized access.

# TODO: check ARCO availability
# arco = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/has_arco/{DSID}/").json()
# print("ARCO available:", arco["data"]["has_arco"])
GDEX provides a REST API to discover and explore hundreds of scientific datasets. Datasets are identified by a six-digit dsid, and most metadata endpoints require no authentication.
See api_intro.ipynb for a deeper look at the API, or zarr_intro.ipynb to start accessing cloud-optimized ARCO data.

GDEX API Documentation

GDEX Dataset Catalog

NSF NCAR Research Data Archive

GDEX allows users to submit custom subset requests — specifying spatial region, time range, and variables — and then download the resulting files. This notebook walks through the full request lifecycle.

Obtain an authentication token from your GDEX profile

Retrieve a control file template for a dataset

Submit a subset request

Poll the request status until complete

Retrieve and download the output files

The GDEX REST API provides programmatic access to dataset discovery, metadata, subset requests, and file downloads. This notebook introduces the API conventions, authentication, and the standard response envelope.

Understand the base URL and endpoint structure

Learn the standard JSON response envelope

Authenticate with a token or Basic auth

Make your first API call

Concepts

Importance

Notes

HTTP basics

Helpful

GET/POST, status codes

requests library

Necessary

Python HTTP client

JSON

Helpful

Parsing responses

Time to learn: 15 minutes

import requests
import json

All endpoints live under https://gdex.ucar.edu/api/. Dataset IDs use the format d###### (six digits). Every successful response follows this envelope:{
  "status": "ok",
  "http_response": 200,
  "error_messages": [],
  "contact": "datahelp@ucar.edu",
  "data": { ... }
}

BASE_URL = "https://gdex.ucar.edu"
# TODO: make a test request to verify connectivity
# resp = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/datasets/")
# print(resp.status_code)

Many read endpoints are public. Subset/download endpoints require authentication via:

Token: ?token=<your_token> — get yours at /accounts/profile/

Basic auth: requests.get(url, auth=("username", "password"))

# TODO: authenticate with your token
# TOKEN = "your_token_here"
# resp = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/status/", params={"token": TOKEN})
# print(resp.json())

GET /api/datasets/ lists all available datasets with their IDs and titles.

# TODO: list all datasets
# resp = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/datasets/")
# envelope = resp.json()
# print(envelope["data"]["count"], "datasets")
# for ds in envelope["data"]["datasets"][:5]:
#     print(ds["id"], "—", ds["title"])
The GDEX API follows a consistent REST pattern: GET https://gdex.ucar.edu/api/<endpoint>/. Public endpoints need no auth; subset and download endpoints require a token from your GDEX profile.
See api_metadata.ipynb to explore dataset metadata in detail, or api_subset.ipynb to submit your first subset request.

GDEX API Documentation

GDEX Profile & Token

requests library docs

GDEX API Documentation

GDEX offers several ways to obtain data files. This notebook introduces the available download methods and explains when to use each one.

Overview of download methods (web interface, API, Globus, POSIX)

Browse web-accessible files via the API

Direct HTTP file download

Concepts

Importance

Notes

gdex_intro

Necessary

Dataset IDs and account setup

requests library

Necessary



pathlib

Helpful

Local file management

Time to learn: 15 minutes

import requests
import json
from pathlib import Path

Method

Best for

Requires

Web interface

Small, one-off downloads

GDEX account

API / HTTP

Scripted downloads

GDEX account + token

Globus

Large volumes, HPC transfers

Globus account

POSIX

NCAR HPC direct access

NCAR account

BASE_URL = "https://gdex.ucar.edu"
DSID = "d633000"

GET /api/datasets/{dsid}/groups/ lists top-level file groups. GET /api/datasets/{dsid}/webfiles/{gindex}/ lists files within a group.

# TODO: list file groups and web-accessible files
# groups = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/datasets/{DSID}/groups/").json()
# for g in groups["data"][:3]:
#     print(g)
# gindex = groups["data"][0]["index"]
# files = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/datasets/{DSID}/webfiles/{gindex}/").json()

Once you have a file URL, use requests to stream the download to disk.

# TODO: download a file
# file_url = "https://gdex.ucar.edu/..."  # from webfiles response
# dest = Path("data") / "myfile.nc"
# dest.parent.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
# with requests.get(file_url, stream=True) as r:
#     with open(dest, "wb") as f:
#         for chunk in r.iter_content(chunk_size=8192):
#             f.write(chunk)
GDEX supports HTTP, Globus, and POSIX access. For scripted bulk downloads, use the API to list files then stream them to disk. For large transfers to HPC, prefer Globus.
See download_workflow.ipynb for a complete end-to-end pattern, or globus_intro.ipynb to use Globus for large transfers.

GDEX Dataset Catalog

GDEX API Documentation

Globus

Submit a subset request via the API

Poll until the request completes

Download all output files

Verify and clean up

Notes

BASE_URL = "https://gdex.ucar.edu"
DSID = "d633000"
# TODO: build and submit your control file

# resp = requests.post(f"{BASE_URL}/api/submit/?token={TOKEN}", json=control_file)
# rindex = resp.json()["data"]["rindex"]
# print("rindex:", rindex)

# TODO: poll request status
# while True:
#     status = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/status/{rindex}/?token={TOKEN}").json()
#     print(status)
#     if "Completed" in str(status): break
#     time.sleep(60)

# TODO: list output files and download each one
# files = requests.get(f"{BASE_URL}/api/get_req_files/{rindex}/?token={TOKEN}").json()
# output_dir = Path("downloads")
# output_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
# for item in files["data"]:
#     # download item to output_dir
#     pass

After downloading, verify file sizes and optionally purge the server-side request with DELETE /api/purge/{rindex}/?token={token}.

# TODO: verify downloads and purge the request
# for f in output_dir.iterdir():
#     print(f.name, f.stat().st_size)
# requests.delete(f"{BASE_URL}/api/purge/{rindex}/?token={TOKEN}")
A complete GDEX download workflow has four steps: submit → poll → download → purge. Wrapping the polling loop in a helper function makes it reusable across multiple requests.
See globus_intro.ipynb to transfer results to an HPC system via Globus instead of HTTP download.

GDEX API Documentation

GDEX Profile & Token

Globus provides high-speed, reliable data transfer between GDEX and your institution or HPC system. The GDEX API can initiate a Globus transfer directly from a completed subset request.

Complete a GDEX subset request

Find your Globus endpoint UUID

Initiate the transfer via the GDEX API

Monitor transfer progress

Before initiating a Globus transfer you need:

A completed GDEX request rindex (see submission.ipynb)

Your destination Globus endpoint UUID (find it at 

app.globus.org)

BASE_URL = "https://gdex.ucar.edu"
# TODO: set your request index and Globus endpoint
# TOKEN    = "your_gdex_token"
# RINDEX   = "your_rindex"
# ENDPOINT = "xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx"  # Globus endpoint UUID

GET /api/globus_download/{rindex}/{endpoint} instructs GDEX to push the completed request files to your Globus endpoint.

# TODO: initiate the Globus transfer

Track transfer progress at 

app​.globus​.org​/activity or use the Globus SDK for programmatic monitoring.

# import globus_sdk
# ...
Globus transfers are the recommended method for moving large GDEX datasets to HPC systems. The GDEX API can initiate the transfer directly from a completed request index.
See download_workflow.ipynb for an HTTP download alternative, or posix_intro.ipynb for direct filesystem access on NCAR HPC.

# ax.set_title("ERA5 minus JRA-3Q: 2-m Temperature")
# plt.show()
Multi-dataset fusion on GDEX requires careful grid alignment. xarray’s interpolation methods handle moderate regridding; for production use consider a dedicated library like xESMF.
See observations.ipynb to fuse model data with in-situ observations, or individual dataset notebooks for access details.

High-resolution datasets (sub-degree grids or convection-permitting scales) require careful chunking strategies to avoid memory issues. This notebook covers patterns for working with large, high-resolution GDEX datasets.

Identify a high-resolution dataset on GDEX

Choose an appropriate access method and chunk strategy

Load a spatial subset lazily

Perform a computationally efficient analysis

Concepts

Importance

Notes

zarr_intro or tds_intro

Necessary

At least one GDEX access method

xarray

Necessary



Dask

Necessary

Chunked/parallel computation

cartopy

Helpful



Time to learn: 35 minutes

import xarray as xr
import numpy as np
import dask
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import cartopy.crs as ccrs

Check spatial resolution via GET /api/datasets/{dsid}/spatial_coverage/. Datasets with sub-0.1° resolution or large grid dimensions require particular care.

# TODO: check spatial resolution
# import requests
# spatial = requests.get("https://gdex.ucar.edu/api/datasets/d######/spatial_coverage/").json()
# res = spatial["data"]["spatial_coverage"]["resolution"]
# print(f"Resolution: {res['latitude']}° x {res['longitude']}°")

For high-resolution data, chunk along time (one time step per chunk) or spatially (tiles) to keep individual chunks manageable in memory.

# TODO: open with appropriate chunking
# ds = xr.open_zarr("https://...", chunks={"time": 1, "latitude": 512, "longitude": 512})
# -- or for OPeNDAP --
# ds = xr.open_dataset("https://...", chunks={"time": 1})

# TODO: subset and compute
# region = ds["var"].sel(latitude=slice(45, 35), longitude=slice(-110, -100))
# result = region.mean(dim="time").compute()

# TODO: plot the result
# fig, ax = plt.subplots(subplot_kw={"projection": ccrs.PlateCarree()})
# result.plot(ax=ax, transform=ccrs.PlateCarree())
# ax.coastlines()
# plt.show()
High-resolution datasets require explicit chunking to prevent memory overflow. Always open lazily, subset before computing, and monitor Dask task graphs to understand data movement.
See zarr_workflow.ipynb for the general ARCO workflow, or data_fusion.ipynb to combine high-res data with coarser reference datasets.
